To use Azure, you need an Azure account. Your Azure account is the credential you use to sign into Azure services like the [Azure portal](https://portal.azure.com) or [Cloud Shell](https://shell.azure.com).
## Option 1: Use monthly Azure credits for Visual Studio subscribers
If you have a Visual Studio subscription, your subscription includes credits for using Azure. Activate your credits by visiting the [Monthly Azure credits for Visual Studio subscribers](https://azure.microsoft.com/pricing/member-offers/credit-for-visual-studio-subscribers/) page.
## Option 2: Sign up for a free Azure account
You can create an [Azure account for free](https://azure.microsoft.com/pricing/purchase-options/azure-account?cid=msft_learn) and receive 12 months of popular services for free and a $200 credit to explore Azure for 30 days.
## Option 3: Sign up for a pay-as-you-go account
You can also create a [pay-as-you-go Azure account](https://azure.microsoft.com/pricing/purchase-options/azure-account?cid=msft_learn). This option includes monthly amounts of select services for free, and charges you for what you use beyond the free limits. There is no upfront commitment and you can cancel anytime.
## Option 4: Use a corporate account
If you are using Azure at work, talk to your company's cloud administrator to get your Azure credentials and then sign in to your account with the [Azure portal](https://portal.azure.com).","contentUrl":"https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/azure/create-azure-account"},{"title":"Try Azure SQL Managed Instance for free","content":"# Try Azure SQL Managed Instance for free
## What's included in the free offer
^1^ Since system files can take up to 32 GB of storage, the available storage for user data might be less than 64 GB. ^2^ Free instances that use the [Next-gen General Purpose](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/azure-sql/managed-instance/service-tiers-next-gen-general-purpose-use?view=azuresql) service tier upgrade are limited to 300 IOPS, and the IOPS slider is unavailable.
Additionally, the following limitations apply:
1. The offer is valid for 12 months from the day you activate the offer. You can apply it to only one instance at a time, per subscription.
2. After 12 months, the free SQL managed instance stops. If you don't upgrade the instance to a paid version within 30 days, the instance and all databases are deleted and no longer recoverable.
3. The following capabilities aren't supported: [Zone redundancy](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/azure-sql/managed-instance/high-availability-sla-local-zone-redundancy?view=azuresql#zone-redundant-availability), [Failover groups](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/azure-sql/managed-instance/failover-group-sql-mi?view=azuresql), [Long-term backup retention](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/azure-sql/database/long-term-retention-overview?view=azuresql), modifying the backup storage redundancy.
4. You can only scale up and down within the free offer limits.
5. The free offer is available in all [regions that support Azure SQL Managed Instance](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/azure-sql/managed-instance/region-availability?view=azuresql).
6. If you delete a free SQL managed instance, you delete all its databases and can't restore them.
### Monthly vCore limits
The monthly free limits include 720 vCore hours of compute. Your free month of credits starts when you create your instance and renews on the same day of the following month.
When you reach the monthly free vCore limit, the instance stops with a status of `Stopped - Insufficient credit`. A banner on the **Overview** page of your instance in the Azure portal gives you the option to create a new paid instance. You can [restore your database](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/azure-sql/managed-instance/point-in-time-restore?view=azuresql#restore-an-existing-database) to the new instance to continue your business without the limits imposed by the free offer.","contentUrl":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/azure-sql/managed-instance/free-offer?view=azuresql#what's-included-in-the-free-offer"},{"title":"Why is my Azure for Students subscription disabled and how do I reactivate it?","content":"# Why is my Azure for Students subscription disabled and how do I reactivate it?
Your Azure for Students subscription might get disabled because you used all of your credit. It might also get disabled if your credit is expired or you accidentally canceled your subscription. See what issue applies to you and learn how you can get your subscription reactivated.
## You used all of your credit
Azure for Students account gives you $100 in credit and a limited quantity of free services for 12 months. Any usage beyond the free services and quantities is deducted from your credit. Once your credit runs out, Azure disables your services and subscription. To continue using Azure services, you must upgrade your subscription to a pay-as-you-go subscription by contacting [Azure support](https://portal.azure.com/?#blade/Microsoft_Azure_Support/HelpAndSupportBlade). After upgrade, your subscription still has access to free services for 12 months from your sign-up date. You only get charged for usage beyond the free services and quantities.
You can check your remaining credit on the [Microsoft Azure Sponsorships portal](https://www.microsoftazuresponsorships.com/balance).
1. Sign in using your Azure for Students account credentials.
2. The balance page gives information about used and remaining credit. You can find your credit expiration date below the credit chart.
 ![Screenshot that shows credit information for Azure for Students subscription.](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/cost-management-billing/manage/media/azurestudents-subscription-disabled/azurestudents-credit-balance.png)
3. To check usage of services that are consuming your Azure credit, select **Usage** from the left navigation then select your Azure for Students subscription.
 ![Screenshot that shows usage information for Azure for Students credit.](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/cost-management-billing/manage/media/azurestudents-subscription-disabled/azurestudents-credit-usage.png)
The table contains the following columns:
1. **Service Name:** Service that is consuming your Azure credit.
2. **Service Resource:** Unit of measurement for the service being consumed.
3. **Spend:** Amount of credit in USD($) spent on the service.","contentUrl":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/cost-management-billing/manage/azurestudents-subscription-disabled"}]}

{"results":[{"title":"Use an Azure free account to try Azure Database for PostgreSQL for free","content":"# Use an Azure free account to try Azure Database for PostgreSQL for free
Azure Database for PostgreSQL is a managed service that you use to run, manage, and scale highly available PostgreSQL databases in the cloud. With an Azure free account, you can use an Azure Database for PostgreSQL flexible server instance for **free for 12 months** with **monthly limits** of up to:
1. **750 hours** of **Burstable B1MS** instance, enough hours to run a database instance continuously each month.
2. **32 GB storage and 32 GB backup storage**.
This article shows you how to create and use an Azure Database for PostgreSQL flexible server instance for free using an [Azure free account](https://azure.microsoft.com/pricing/purchase-options/azure-account?cid=msft_learn).
## Prerequisites
To complete this tutorial, you need:
1. An Azure free account. If you don't have one, [create a free account](https://azure.microsoft.com/pricing/purchase-options/azure-account?cid=msft_learn) before you begin.","contentUrl":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/postgresql/configure-maintain/how-to-deploy-on-azure-free-account"},{"title":"Use an Azure free account to try Azure Database for PostgreSQL for free","content":"# Use an Azure free account to try Azure Database for PostgreSQL for free
## Monitor and track free services usage
You're not charged for Azure Database for PostgreSQL services that are included for free with your Azure free account unless you exceed the free service limits. To remain within the limits, use the Azure portal to track and monitor your free services usage.

3.1. Meter: Identifies the unit of measure for the service being consumed.
3.2. Usage/Limit: Current month's usage and limit for the meter.
3.3. Status: Usage status of the service. Based on your usage, you can have one of the following statutes:
3.4. Not in use: You haven't used the meter or the usage for the meter hasn't reached the billing system.
3.5. Exceeded on <Date>: You've exceeded the limit for the meter on <Date>.

The admin username must contain between 1 and 63 characters, must only contain numbers and letters, can't start with **pg\_** and can't be **azure\_superuser**, **azure\_pg\_admin**, **admin**, **administrator**, **root**, **guest**, or **public**. |

It must also contain characters from three of the following four categories: English uppercase letters, English lowercase letters, numbers (0 through 9), and non-alphanumeric characters (!, $, #, %, and so on). ## Create a flexible server instance
When creating the Azure Database for PostgreSQL flexible server instance using your Azure free account, you will see an **Estimated cost per month** in the **Compute + Storage: Cost Summary** blade and the **Review + Create** tab. However, as long as you stay within the monthly limits of your Azure free account (refer to the **Monitor and track free services usage** section below for usage information). There is no charge for the service. We are currently working to improve the **Cost Summary** experience for free services.
11. Select **Create** to provision the server.
Provisioning can take a few minutes.
12. On the toolbar, select **Notifications** (the bell icon) to monitor the deployment process.
After the deployment is complete, select **Pin to dashboard**, to create a tile for the Azure Database for PostgreSQL flexible server instance on your Azure portal dashboard. This tile is a shortcut to the server's **Overview** page. When you select **Go to resource**, the server's **Overview** page opens.
By default, a **postgres** database is created under your server. The postgres database is a default database that's meant for use by users, utilities, and third-party applications. (The other default database is **azure\_maintenance**. Its function is to separate the managed service processes from user actions. You can't access this database.)
## Connect and query
Now that you've created an Azure Database for PostgreSQL flexible server instance in a resource group, you can connect to server and query databases by using the following Connect and query quickstarts:
1. [psql](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/postgresql/configure-maintain/quickstart-create-server#connect-using-psql).
2. [Azure CLI](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/postgresql/connectivity/connect-azure-cli).

4. [Java](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/postgresql/connectivity/connect-java).
5. [.NET](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/postgresql/connectivity/connect-csharp).

## Monitor and track free services usage
With an Azure free account, you also get $200 in credit to use in 30 days. During this time, any usage beyond the free monthly amounts of services will be deducted from this credit. At the end of your first 30 days or after you spend your $200 credit (whichever comes first), you'll only pay for what you use beyond the free monthly amounts of services. To keep getting free services after 30 days, move to pay-as-you-go pricing. If you don't move to pay as you go, you can't purchase Azure services beyond your $200 credit—and eventually your account and services will be disabled. For more information, see [**Azure free account FAQ**](https://azure.microsoft.com/free/free-account-faq/).
## Clean up resources
If you're using the Azure Database for PostgreSQL flexible server instance for development, testing, or predictable, time-bound production workloads, optimize usage by starting and stopping the server on-demand. After you stop the server, it remains in that state for seven days unless restarted sooner. For more information, see Start/Stop Server to lower TCO. When your Azure Database for PostgreSQL flexible server instance is stopped, there's no Compute usage, but Storage usage is still considered.
Alternatively, if you don't expect to need these resources in the future, you can delete them by deleting the resource group, or you can just delete the Azure Database for PostgreSQL flexible server instance.

1.3. On the **Overview** page for your resource group, select **Delete resource group.**
1.4. In the confirmation dialog box, type the name of your resource group, and then select **Delete**.
2. The server name can contain only lowercase letters, numbers, and the hyphen (-) character. It must contain between 3 and 63 characters. |

Azure Database for PostgreSQL is a managed service that you can use to run, manage, and scale highly available PostgreSQL databases in the cloud.
This quickstart shows you how to create an Azure Database for PostgreSQL flexible server instance by using the Azure portal, Azure CLI, or Azure Resource Manager (ARM) templates.
## Prerequisites
Before you begin, make sure you have:
1. An Azure subscription. If you don't have one, create a [free Azure account](https://azure.microsoft.com/pricing/purchase-options/azure-account?cid=msft_learn).
2. Appropriate permissions to create resources in your subscription.
## Understand what you're creating
An Azure Database for PostgreSQL flexible server instance includes:
1. A configured set of [compute and storage resources](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/postgresql/configure-maintain/concepts-compute).
2. Deployment within an [Azure resource group](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/azure-resource-manager/management/overview).
3. A `postgres` database created by default.
4. An `azure_maintenance` database for managed service processes.
5. An `azure_sys` database for query store and autonomous tuning features.
Note
Connections typically use port 5432, or port 6432 if connecting through the built-in [PgBouncer](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/postgresql/connectivity/concepts-pgbouncer) connection pooler.","contentUrl":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/postgresql/configure-maintain/quickstart-create-server"},{"title":"Storage in Azure Database for PostgreSQL flexible server","content":"# Storage in Azure Database for PostgreSQL flexible server

During the first 30 days after you've created an Azure free account, you have $200 credit in your billing currency to use on any service, except for third-party Marketplace purchases. You can experiment with different tiers and types of Azure services using the free credit to try out Azure. If you use services or Azure resources that aren’t free during that time, charges are deducted against your credit.
If you don’t use all of your credit by the end of the first 30 days, it's lost. After the first 30 days and up to 12 months after sign-up, you can only use a limited quantity of *some services*—not all Azure services are free. If you upgrade before 30 days and have remaining credit, you can use the rest of your credit with a pay-as-you-go subscription for the remaining days. For example, if you sign up for the free account on November 1 and upgrade on November 5, you have until November 30 to use your credit in the new pay-as-you-go subscription.
Your Azure free account includes a *specified quantity* of free services for 12 months and a set of services that are always free. Only some tiers of services are available for free within certain quantities. For example, Azure has many virtual machines intended for different needs. The free account includes access to three types of VMs for free—the B1S, B2pts v2 (ARM-based), and B2ats v2 (AMD-based) burstable VMs that are usable for up to 750 hours per month. By staying in the free account limits, you can use the free services in various configurations. For more information about the Azure free account and the products that are available for free, see [Azure free account FAQ](https://azure.microsoft.com/pricing/purchase-options/azure-account?cid=msft_learn).","contentUrl":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/cost-management-billing/manage/create-free-services"},{"title":"Storage in Azure Database for PostgreSQL flexible server","content":"# Storage in Azure Database for PostgreSQL flexible server
## Differences between Premium SSD and Premium SSD v2
Unlike Premium SSD, Premium SSD v2 doesn't have dedicated sizes. You can set a Premium SSD v2 disk to any size you prefer, and make granular adjustments as per your workload requirements. Those granular increments can go in steps of 1 GiB. Premium SSD v2 doesn't support host caching, but still provide lower latency than Premium SSD. Premium SSD v2 capacities range from 1 GiB to 64 TiBs.
The following table provides a comparison of different aspect of the types of disk supported by Azure Database for PostgreSQL flexible server instances, to help you decide which one suits your needs better.
| - | Premium SSD v2 | Premium SSD| 
|  --- | --- | ---  |
| **Disk type** | SSD | SSD |
| **Scenario** | Production and performance-sensitive workloads that consistently require low latency and high IOPS and throughput. | Production and performance-sensitive workloads. |
| **Max disk size** | 65,536 GiB | 32,767 GiB |
| **Max throughput** | 1,200 MB/s | 900 MB/s |
| **Max IOPS** | 80,000 | 20,000 |

Premium SSD v2 offers up to 32 TiBs per region per subscription by default, but supports higher capacity by request. To request an increase in capacity, request a quota increase or contact [Azure Support](https://ms.portal.azure.com/#view/Microsoft_Azure_Support/HelpAndSupportBlade/%7E/overview).
Azure Database for PostgreSQL service offers a baseline throughput of 125 MB/s for disks up to 399 GiB, and 500 MB/s for disks over 400 GiB at no extra cost. Increasing throughput beyond the free tier results in extra charges.
The storage that you provision is the amount of storage capacity available to your Azure Database for PostgreSQL flexible server instance. This storage is used for database files, temporary files, transaction logs, and PostgreSQL server logs. The total amount of storage that you provision also defines the I/O capacity available to your server.","contentUrl":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/postgresql/compute-storage/concepts-storage#differences-between-premium-ssd-and-premium-ssd-v2"}]}

content.text:
PR #598 is merged and `main` is up to date. Now the Azure question — I verified the terms against Microsoft's current docs so this isn't from memory. Short version: **day 30 is only a cliff if you don't upgrade; the "1 year" part only kicks in if you do.**

## How the offer actually works

The free account is two separate things:

1. **$200 credit, first 30 days only.** Everything you run is burned against it. Unused credit is lost at day 30 — it never carries past that date, even if you upgrade.
2. **12 months of limited free service quantities** (+ some always-free services). This is the "1 year" — but it only continues **if you upgrade to pay-as-you-go**. Upgrading requires adding a card; you're then charged only for usage *beyond* the free quantities.

**If you do nothing at day 30:** the subscription is **disabled** — frontend, api, worker, Redis, Postgres, ADF polling all stop at once. Resources aren't deleted immediately (there's a grace window where upgrading re-enables them), but the app is effectively dead. One important survivor: **the Entra tenant is not part of the subscription** — the demo users and app registrations keep working, so Azure-AD login for local dev survives even a disabled subscription.

## What DataQ would cost on pay-as-you-go

| DataQ piece | After upgrade |
|---|---|
| Postgres (`dataq-pg-wus3-*`, shared app+harness) | **Free for 12 months** if it's B1MS with ≤32GB — 750 h/month covers 24/7. This is the big one; worth confirming the SKU |
| Container Apps ×4 (frontend, api, worker, redis) | Always-free grant: 180K vCPU-s + 360K GiB-s + 2M requests/month per subscription. A month is ~2.6M seconds, so our always-on worker+redis exceed the grant → the main real cost. Idle-rate billing helps (a quiet worker between beats bills reduced); ballpark **$10–40/month**, less if api scales to zero and sizes are trimmed |
| App Insights / Log Analytics | First 5GB/month ingestion free — our volume is likely within it |
| Key Vault, ADLS, ADF (harness) | Pennies; ADF is per-activity-run — pause the harness triggers if not demoing |

## What this means for the v1.1 plan

The W1–3 sequencing treated ~07-25 as a hard stop. Upgrading changes the calculus:

- **If you upgrade (~$10–40/month):** the Azure deadline disappears. Prod stays live through the whole cycle — which means live E2E for the schema_drift/anomaly/scale weeks too — and W3's #590 becomes a **cost-trim pass** (scale-to-zero, pause harness ADF, prune ingestion) instead of `terraform destroy`. Bonus: upgrading lifts the trial quota caps (the 1-env/1-PG-server limit that forced the shared-server design in ADR 0024). If you go this way: set a **Cost Management budget + alert** (say $30/month) immediately — PAYG has no spend cap.
- **If you don't:** the current plan is exactly right — everything cloud-verified by end of W3, deliberate teardown before the disable hits, and note that whatever's left of the $200 evaporates at day 30 regardless (an argument for *spending* it on the #587 volume baseline and W2 live verification rather than conserving it).

My take: the Snowflake urgency is unchanged either way (separate subscription, days left). For Azure, if a small monthly bill is acceptable, upgrading before day 30 is clearly better value than racing the teardown — but it's a real card-on-file decision, so it's yours. If you decide, I'll adjust #590/#591 and the W3 milestone accordingly.
Can you check the actual cost we will pay [harness jobs on and off] against the actual skus
Good idea — let me pull the actual deployed SKUs and usage from the subscription rather than estimating generically. First, inventory what's actually running and billable.

content.text:
I now have the full picture from the actual Terraform (app stack + harness). One caveat up front: the pricing page hides exact numbers behind JavaScript, so I'm using the standard US-region ACA consumption list rates (≈$63/month per always-active vCPU, ≈$7.90/month per always-idle vCPU, ≈$7.90/month per GiB — idle discount applies to CPU only, memory bills the same either way). Also note: **the local `az` CLI is logged in as demo user Olivia** (leftover from the go-live workout) with Reader on one resource only, so I couldn't pull your real meter data — these are computed from the deployed sizes.

## What's actually deployed (from Terraform)

**App stack — all `min_replicas ≥ 1`, so always allocated:**

| App | Size | Always-on |
|---|---|---|
| `dataq-app-worker` (Celery + beat) | 1.0 vCPU / 2 GiB | yes (beat can't scale to zero) |
| `dataq-app-api` | 0.5 / 1 GiB (max 3) | yes |
| `dataq-app-redis` | 0.5 / 1 GiB | yes |
| `dataq-app-frontend` | 0.25 / 0.5 GiB (max 2) | yes |
| migrate job | 0.5 / 1 GiB | per-run, negligible |

Total: **2.25 vCPU / 4.5 GiB allocated 24/7.**

**Harness adds — this is the surprise:**

| App | Size | Always-on |
|---|---|---|
| Airflow scheduler+webserver | 2.0 vCPU / 4 GiB | yes |
| Airflow workers | 2.0 / 4 GiB **× min 2** | yes — **4.0 vCPU / 8 GiB** |
| harness redis | 0.5 / 1 GiB | yes |
| mockdata cron jobs, ADF (2 triggers), Event Grid, ADLS | — | pennies |

Total: **+6.5 vCPU / +13 GiB always allocated — the harness is ~3× the app's compute.**

## Monthly cost estimate (pay-as-you-go, after free grants)

| Scenario | All-idle (best) | Realistic† | All-active (worst) |
|---|---|---|---|
| App only (harness off) | ~$50 | **~$100–140** | ~$175 |
| Harness only | ~$155 | **~$350–450** | ~$515 |
| **Total, harness on** | ~$205 | **~$450–560** | ~$690 |

†"Realistic" assumes the Celery worker, both redises, the Airflow scheduler and its 2 workers bill *active* (constant broker polling/heartbeats usually exceed the 1,000 bytes/s idle threshold), while api/frontend/webserver sit idle. The idle-vs-active classification is the single biggest uncertainty — only the real bill settles it.

Everything else is roughly free: **Postgres is $0** (`B_Standard_B1ms` + 32 GB is exactly the 12-month free quantity, 750 h/month), Log Analytics likely under the 5 GB free, ADF ~$2–5, KV/Event Grid/storage/bandwidth ~$1–2.

## The urgent implication

If the harness has been fully up since deploy, burn is plausibly **$15–20/day — meaning the $200 credit could run out in days, well before the 30-day mark** (services get disabled at credit exhaustion, not at day 30). I'd check the real balance today: portal → Subscriptions → your Free Trial → credit remaining. If you `az login` as the owner account, I can pull Cost Management month-to-date and replace these estimates with actuals.

## The levers, in order of impact

1. **Scale the harness to zero when not demoing** (Airflow workers min 2→0, scheduler/webserver min 1→0, harness redis off): saves ~$350–450/month — by far the biggest lever, and reversible in one `az containerapp update` / TF variable per app.
2. **Trim the app**: api min 1→0 (cold starts behind the nginx proxy are the tradeoff), worker 1.0→0.5 vCPU (keep 2 GiB for GX/pandas headroom): app drops to roughly **$50–75/month** — that's the realistic steady-state bill if you upgrade to PAYG and keep prod alive through the cycle.
3. Requests never matter here (2M/month free, health probes don't count).

So the upgrade decision from earlier now has real numbers: **harness-off + trimmed app ≈ $50–75/month** to keep prod alive past day 30; harness-on continuously is ~$500/month and isn't worth it — wake it for demo/E2E windows only. Want me to fold a "scale-to-zero harness + app trim" task into #590/#591?
I logged in using royarijit04, can you ncheck once more

content.text:
Full actuals now, from Cost Management (your billing currency is CAD). The picture is sharp:

## Real burn — CAD $21.69/day average (July 1–3), $144.80 consumed since the June 27 deploy

| Resource | CAD/day | Share |
|---|---|---|
| `dataq-harness-airflow` (scheduler+webserver, 2 vCPU/4Gi) | 8.99 | ← biggest single line |
| `dataq-harness-airflow-worker` (2× 2 vCPU/4Gi) | 6.92 | |
| `dataq-harness-redis` + `airflow-trigger` + ADF | 1.50 | |
| **Harness subtotal** | **~17.41** | **80%** |
| `dataq-app-worker` / `api` / `redis` / `frontend` | 4.04 | 19% |
| ACR (Basic, base fee) | 0.24 | |
| Postgres B1ms, Key Vault, logs, storage, mockdata jobs, alerts | **0.00** | free tiers doing their job |

So monthly: **harness ON ≈ CAD $660/mo (~USD $480)** · **harness OFF ≈ CAD $130/mo (~USD $95)** — my Terraform-based estimate held up, and the split answers your question directly: **the harness is 80% of the bill; the app itself is ~$4/day.**

## The runway problem

The Canada-localized free credit is normally **CAD $260** (the "$200" USD grant — please confirm the exact balance in portal → Subscriptions → Free Trial, the API doesn't expose it). If so:

- **Remaining ≈ CAD $115** → at $21.7/day the credit dies **~July 9–10** — not "3 weeks from now." The subscription gets disabled at credit exhaustion, not at day 30.
- **Scale the harness down today** and burn drops to ~$4.3/day → the same $115 lasts **~26 days**, comfortably past the day-30 mark (~July 26). The W1–3 plan timeline survives without paying anything.

## Recommendation

Stop the four harness apps now (`az containerapp stop` on `dataq-harness-airflow`, `-airflow-worker`, `-airflow-trigger`, `-redis` — fully reversible with `start` for demo/E2E windows). Two caveats: DataQ's 10-min Airflow polling will log connection failures while it's down (possible #419 alert noise — snooze or disable the Airflow connection's suites), and the Flow B/C harness legs are dead until restarted. ADF ($0.27/day) can stay.

This also refines the earlier upgrade math: post-upgrade PAYG steady state is **~CAD $130/mo as-deployed, likely ~CAD $60–80 trimmed** (api scale-to-zero, worker 0.5 vCPU), with the harness woken only when needed.

Want me to stop the harness apps now and fold this (credit-runway note + harness-wake runbook) into #590/#591?
Yeah, stop the harness jobs and lets try to wrap it up by 30 days mark
On it — stopping the harness compute, quieting its ADF triggers, and locking the decision into the plan.

**Cost actuals that drove it** (Cost Management, billing currency CAD): burn was **CAD ~21.7/day** (~660/mo), of which the harness was **80%** — \`dataq-harness-airflow\` 8.99/day + 2× \`airflow-worker\` 6.92/day + redis/trigger/ADF ~1.50/day; the app itself is only ~4.04/day; ACR Basic 0.24/day; Postgres B1ms/32GB = \$0 (12-month free SKU). **CAD 144.80 of the free credit was already consumed by Jul 4** — at the old rate the credit would have died ~Jul 9–10, well before day 30. With the harness stopped, burn drops to ~CAD 4.5/day and the remaining credit (~115 if the grant is the standard CAD 260 — *verify balance in portal → Subscriptions → Free Trial*) comfortably reaches day 30. **W3's due date (2026-07-25) is now a hard wrap-up deadline.**

**Stopped/suspended today (all reversible):**
- \`az containerapp stop\`(ARM \`/stop\`): \`dataq-harness-airflow\`, \`dataq-harness-airflow-worker\`, \`dataq-harness-airflow-trigger\`, \`dataq-harness-redis\` — verified \`Stopped\`; the 4 \`dataq-app-*\` apps untouched and \`Running\` (frontend 200 ✓)

input.content:
---
name: project-azure-winddown
description: Azure free-trial wind-down — harness compute STOPPED 2026-07-04, no PAYG upgrade, hard wrap-up by day-30 (~2026-07-26); cost actuals + wake runbook on #590
metadata:
  type: project
---

Decision (2026-07-04): **no pay-as-you-go upgrade** — DataQ wraps up its Azure presence by
the free-trial day-30 mark (**~2026-07-26**; W3 milestone due 2026-07-25 is the hard
deadline for #590 teardown + #591 local-first posture). Snowflake subscription lapses
separately ~2026-07-05..10 (v1.1 W1, #588).

**Why:** Cost Management actuals (billing currency **CAD**): burn was ~21.7/day
(harness = 80% — Airflow scheduler 8.99 + 2×2-vCPU workers 6.92/day); CAD 144.80 of the
free credit already consumed by Jul 4 → credit would have died ~Jul 9–10. Postgres
B1ms/32GB is $0 (12-month free SKU). App-only burn ~CAD 4.5/day.

**State since 2026-07-04:** the 4 harness container apps (`dataq-harness-airflow`,
`-airflow-worker`, `-airflow-trigger`, `-redis`) are **Stopped**; both ADF triggers
(`tr_customers_daily`, `tr_orders_landed`) **Stopped**; 5 `mockdata-*` cron jobs
**suspended** (api-version `2024-08-02-preview`). The 4 `dataq-app-*` apps still run.
Flows B/C dead until woken; DataQ's 10-min Airflow poll logs failures (noise only).
**Wake runbook is in the #590 comment** (start redis → airflow → worker → trigger;
~CAD 17/day while awake).

**How to apply:** don't treat stopped harness apps / failing Airflow polls as incidents;
before any live E2E, wake per the runbook and stop again after. `az` CLI: was left logged
in as demo user Olivia after go-live; owner login is royarijit04@outlook.com (relevant to
[[project-live-connections-and-demo]] credential hygiene at teardown).

Done — the harness is stopped and the wind-down decision is locked in. Summary:

**Stopped today (all reversible, verified):**
- Container apps `dataq-harness-airflow`, `-airflow-worker`, `-airflow-trigger`, `-redis` → **Stopped**; the four `dataq-app-*` apps untouched and Running (frontend probed 200 ✓)
- ADF triggers `tr_customers_daily` + `tr_orders_landed` → **Stopped**
- The 5 `mockdata-*` cron jobs → **suspended**

**Effect on the money:** burn drops from ~CAD 21.7/day to ~**CAD 4.5/day**. With CAD 144.80 already consumed, the remaining credit (~115 if the grant is the standard CAD 260) now comfortably reaches the day-30 mark (~July 26) instead of dying next week. Worth doing two checks yourself: the exact credit balance in portal → Subscriptions → Free Trial (the API doesn't expose it), and a glance at tomorrow's cost to confirm the drop landed.

**Recorded:** the full decision + cost actuals + a **wake runbook** (redis → airflow → worker → trigger, ~CAD 17/day while awake) as a comment on [#590](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/590), the app-only cost datapoint on [#591](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/591), and a cross-session memory note so future sessions treat stopped harness apps and failing Airflow polls as expected state, not incidents.

**What this means for the plan:** the W3 milestone due date (2026-07-25) is now the hard wrap-up deadline — final live E2E (wake the harness for it, then stop it again), the #492 decision, #590 teardown, and #591 local-first posture all land by then. Two side-findings for the teardown's hygiene pass: local `az` had been sitting logged in as demo user Olivia since the go-live workout, and the ACR (`dataqacr3erlgd`, Basic) is a small standing cost the Terraform teardown should catch.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# harness_window.sh — open/close an ad-hoc DataQ harness test window on Azure.
#
# The harness compute was stopped 2026-07-04 (cost wind-down, DataQ #590): while
# awake it burns ~CAD 17/day (~0.70/hour); stopped it costs ~0. This script wakes
# it, optionally kicks the data flows, and puts it back to sleep.
#
# Usage:
#   harness_window.sh status           # show app/trigger/job states (read-only)
#   harness_window.sh start            # wake: redis -> airflow -> worker -> trigger, ADF triggers on, jobs resumed
#   harness_window.sh run [--adf]      # kick the 5 mockdata jobs (+ optionally the Flow-A ADF pipelines*)
#   harness_window.sh stop             # sleep: triggers off, jobs suspended, apps stopped (reverse order)
#   harness_window.sh window [--adf]   # start -> run -> stop, one shot
#
# *--adf create-runs pl_flow_a_customers + pl_flow_a_orders. Flow A loads
#  SNOWFLAKE — after the Snowflake subscription lapses (v1.1 W1, #588) these runs
#  will fail; the mockdata jobs (file landing) keep working regardless.
#  Airflow DAGs are NOT triggered here — they fire on their own cron while the
#  scheduler is awake (or trigger manually in the Airflow UI).
set -euo pipefail

SUB="37e710fd-b956-4a2e-94f2-b132bb26b3d6"
RG="dataq-rg"
ADF="dataq-harness-adf"
ARM="https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/$SUB/resourceGroups/$RG/providers"
APP_API="2024-03-01"
JOB_API="2024-08-02-preview"
AIRFLOW_URL="https://dataq-harness-airflow.purplefield-f7322a1b.westus2.azurecontainerapps.io"

# Wake order matters: broker first, scheduler before workers. Stop is the reverse.
APPS_START=(dataq-harness-redis dataq-harness-airflow dataq-harness-airflow-worker dataq-harness-airflow-trigger)
APPS_STOP=(dataq-harness-airflow-trigger dataq-harness-airflow-worker dataq-harness-airflow dataq-harness-redis)
TRIGGERS=(tr_customers_daily tr_orders_landed)
JOBS=(mockdata-orders mockdata-inventory mockdata-tracking mockdata-feedback mockdata-supply)

log() { printf '%s %s\n' "$(date +%H:%M:%S)" "$*"; }

app_state() { az containerapp show -n "$1" -g "$RG" --query properties.runningStatus -o tsv 2>/dev/null; }

wait_app() { # wait_app <name> <wanted-state>
  local name=$1 want=$2 i
  for i in $(seq 1 60); do
    [[ "$(app_state "$name")" == "$want" ]] && { log "  $name -> $want"; return 0; }
    sleep 5
  done
  log "  TIMEOUT: $name never reached $want"; return 1
}

cmd_status() {
  az containerapp list -g "$RG" --query "[?starts_with(name,'dataq-harness')].{name:name, running:properties.runningStatus}" -o table
  az datafactory trigger list -g "$RG" --factory-name "$ADF" --query "[].{name:name, state:properties.runtimeState}" -o table
  local j
  for j in "${JOBS[@]}"; do
    printf '%s last execution: ' "$j"
    az rest --method GET --url "$ARM/Microsoft.App/jobs/$j/executions?api-version=$JOB_API" -o json 2>/dev/null |
      python3 -c "import json,sys; v=json.load(sys.stdin)['value']; print(v[0]['properties']['status']+' @ '+v[0]['properties']['startTime'] if v else 'none')"
  done
}

cmd_start() {
  local app t j
  log "Waking harness (billing ~CAD 0.70/hour while awake)..."
  for app in "${APPS_START[@]}"; do
    az rest --method POST --url "$ARM/Microsoft.App/containerApps/$app/start?api-version=$APP_API" -o none 2>/dev/null || true
    wait_app "$app" Running
    if [[ "$app" == "dataq-harness-airflow" ]]; then
      log "  waiting for Airflow /health..."
      for _ in $(seq 1 60); do
        curl -sf --max-time 10 "$AIRFLOW_URL/health" >/dev/null 2>&1 && { log "  Airflow healthy"; break; }
        sleep 10
      done
    fi
  done
  for t in "${TRIGGERS[@]}"; do
    az datafactory trigger start -g "$RG" --factory-name "$ADF" --name "$t" >/dev/null && log "  ADF trigger $t -> Started"
  done
  for j in "${JOBS[@]}"; do
    az rest --method POST --url "$ARM/Microsoft.App/jobs/$j/resume?api-version=$JOB_API" -o none 2>/dev/null && log "  job $j resumed" || true
  done
  log "Harness is UP. Remember to '$0 stop' when done."
}

cmd_run() {
  local with_adf=${1:-} j execs=()
  log "Starting the 5 mockdata job executions (parallel)..."
  for j in "${JOBS[@]}"; do
    local ename
    ename=$(az rest --method POST --url "$ARM/Microsoft.App/jobs/$j/start?api-version=$JOB_API" -o json 2>/dev/null | python3 -c "import json,sys; print(json.load(sys.stdin).get('name',''))")
    log "  $j -> execution ${ename:-<unknown>}"
    execs+=("$j:$ename")
  done
  log "Waiting for job executions to finish (timeout 35m each)..."
  local pair status
  for pair in "${execs[@]}"; do
    j=${pair%%:*}; local e=${pair#*:}
    [[ -z "$e" ]] && continue
    for _ in $(seq 1 70); do
      status=$(az rest --method GET --url "$ARM/Microsoft.App/jobs/$j/executions/$e?api-version=$JOB_API" -o json 2>/dev/null | python3 -c "import json,sys; print(json.load(sys.stdin)['properties']['status'])" 2>/dev/null || echo Unknown)
      [[ "$status" != "Running" && "$status" != "Unknown" ]] && break
      sleep 30
    done
    log "  $j: $status"
  done
  if [[ "$with_adf" == "--adf" ]]; then
    log "Triggering Flow-A ADF pipelines (needs live Snowflake!)..."
    local p rid
    for p in pl_flow_a_customers pl_flow_a_orders; do
      rid=$(az datafactory pipeline create-run -g "$RG" --factory-name "$ADF" --name "$p" --query runId -o tsv)
      log "  $p -> run $rid"
      for _ in $(seq 1 60); do
        status=$(az datafactory pipeline-run show -g "$RG" --factory-name "$ADF" --run-id "$rid" --query status -o tsv)
        [[ "$status" != "InProgress" && "$status" != "Queued" ]] && break
        sleep 15
      done
      log "  $p: $status"
    done
  fi
  log "Run phase done. Airflow DAGs fire on their own cron while awake (or trigger in the UI: $AIRFLOW_URL)."
}

cmd_stop() {
  local t j app
  log "Putting the harness back to sleep..."
  for t in "${TRIGGERS[@]}"; do
    az datafactory trigger stop -g "$RG" --factory-name "$ADF" --name "$t" >/dev/null && log "  ADF trigger $t -> Stopped"
  done
  for j in "${JOBS[@]}"; do
    az rest --method POST --url "$ARM/Microsoft.App/jobs/$j/suspend?api-version=$JOB_API" -o none 2>/dev/null && log "  job $j suspended" || true
  done
  for app in "${APPS_STOP[@]}"; do
    az rest --method POST --url "$ARM/Microsoft.App/containerApps/$app/stop?api-version=$APP_API" -o none 2>/dev/null || true
    wait_app "$app" Stopped
  done
  log "Harness is DOWN (burn back to ~CAD 4.5/day, app only)."
}

case "${1:-}" in
  status) cmd_status ;;
  start)  cmd_start ;;
  run)    cmd_run "${2:-}" ;;
  stop)   cmd_stop ;;
  window) cmd_start; cmd_run "${2:-}"; cmd_stop ;;
  *) grep '^#' "$0" | sed 's/^# \{0,1\}//' | head -20; exit 1 ;;
esac

content.text:
Done. The script is at **`~/Coding/Python/DataQ-harness/scripts/harness_window.sh`** (harness-side, deliberately not in the repo per ADR 0021). For ad-hoc testing you just call:

```bash
~/Coding/Python/DataQ-harness/scripts/harness_window.sh window        # start → run flows → stop, one shot
~/Coding/Python/DataQ-harness/scripts/harness_window.sh window --adf  # same, + Flow-A ADF pipelines (needs live Snowflake)
```

Or the pieces individually: `status` (read-only), `start`, `run [--adf]`, `stop`.

What it does:

- **`start`** wakes things in dependency order — redis → Airflow scheduler (waits for `/health` to go green) → workers → trigger app — then starts both ADF triggers and resumes the 5 mockdata cron jobs. It reminds you it's billing (~CAD 0.70/hour while awake).
- **`run`** kicks all 5 mockdata job executions in parallel and polls each to completion (they land fresh files, which the ADF blob trigger picks up while awake). `--adf` additionally `create-run`s `pl_flow_a_customers` + `pl_flow_a_orders` and polls them — gated behind a flag because Flow A loads Snowflake, which dies this week (#588). Airflow DAGs aren't force-triggered; they fire on their own cron while the scheduler is awake (the script prints the UI URL if you want to trigger one manually).
- **`stop`** reverses everything: ADF triggers off, jobs suspended, apps stopped in reverse order, each verified `Stopped`.

Verification status, honestly: `bash -n` clean, and `status` ran against the live subscription — it correctly shows all four apps `Stopped`, both triggers `Stopped`, and the last (pre-suspension) job executions. The stop/suspend/trigger/executions ARM calls are the exact ones proven earlier today; the only paths not yet exercised are app `/start` and the Airflow health wait, since running them would wake the harness and spend credit. First real use — likely the W3 final live E2E, or your next ad-hoc test — is the true end-to-end validation; if `start` misbehaves I'd suspect only the health-wait timing, not the calls.

Also recorded: a pointer comment on [#590](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/590) (the script supersedes the manual runbook there) and the cross-session memory note now references the script path.
the schedule jobs will run as manual with this script right?

Mostly yes, with one exception worth spelling out — and one edge I can verify right now for a fraction of a cent.

- **Mockdata jobs (the 5 ACA cron jobs): yes.** `run` fires them via the on-demand `start` endpoint — they execute immediately as manual executions, no waiting for their cron. The cron itself is also resumed during a window, so if a job's scheduled time happens to land inside your window it can *additionally* fire once on schedule.
- **ADF (`--adf`): yes.** `create-run` is a manual pipeline run, bypassing the daily schedule trigger (which, in a short window, would likely never fire on its own). Note the blob-event trigger is live during the window, so `pl_flow_a_orders` may also fire automatically when the mockdata files land — you can see it twice.
- **Airflow DAGs: no — the exception.** The script doesn't force-trigger DAG runs; they only fire if their cron lands inside the window (or you trigger manually in the Airflow UI). I can add a `--dags` flag that POSTs to the Airflow REST API (`/api/v1/dags/{id}/dagRuns`) if you want full one-shot coverage — it needs the Airflow credential from Key Vault.

DataQ-side: manual runs are ingested exactly like scheduled ones (the 10-min poll picks up all runs regardless of how they were triggered), so trigger bindings still fire on success.

One edge to verify: whether a manual `start` works while a job is **suspended** (matters if someone calls `run` without `start` first). Let me test that now — one mockdata job, costs ~a cent, no downstream fires since the blob trigger is stopped.

input.old_string:
cmd_run() {
  local with_adf=${1:-} j execs=()
input.new_string:
cmd_dags() {
  local user pass d rid state
  user=$(terraform -chdir="$TF_DIR" output -raw airflow_admin_user)
  pass=$(terraform -chdir="$TF_DIR" output -raw airflow_admin_password)
  log "Triggering the cron DAGs via the Airflow REST API (sequential)..."
  for d in "${DAGS[@]}"; do
    # a dagRun created on a paused DAG sits queued forever -> unpause first (idempotent)
    curl -sf --max-time 30 -X PATCH "$AIRFLOW_URL/api/v1/dags/$d?update_mask=is_paused" \
      -H 'Content-Type: application/json' --user "$user:$pass" -d '{"is_paused": false}' >/dev/null
    rid=$(curl -sf --max-time 30 -X POST "$AIRFLOW_URL/api/v1/dags/$d/dagRuns" \
      -H 'Content-Type: application/json' --user "$user:$pass" -d '{}' |
      python3 -c "import json,sys; print(json.load(sys.stdin)['dag_run_id'])")
    log "  $d -> $rid"
    for _ in $(seq 1 60); do
      state=$(curl -sf --max-time 30 --user "$user:$pass" \
        "$AIRFLOW_URL/api/v1/dags/$d/dagRuns/$rid" |
        python3 -c "import json,sys; print(json.load(sys.stdin)['state'])" 2>/dev/null || echo unknown)
      [[ "$state" != "queued" && "$state" != "running" && "$state" != "unknown" ]] && break
      sleep 15
    done
    log "  $d: $state"
  done
}

cmd_run() {
  local with_adf="" with_dags="" a j execs=()
  for a in "$@"; do
    [[ "$a" == "--adf" ]] && with_adf=1
    [[ "$a" == "--dags" ]] && with_dags=1
  done

!/usr/bin/env bash
harness_window.sh — open/close an ad-hoc DataQ harness test window on Azure.

The harness compute was stopped 2026-07-04 (cost wind-down, DataQ #590): while
awake it burns ~CAD 17/day (~0.70/hour); stopped it costs ~0. This script wakes
it, optionally kicks the data flows, and puts it back to sleep.

content.text:
Done — here's what the Airflow docs check found and what changed in the script.

**Docs findings (Airflow 2.10.5, the harness's exact version):**
- The stable REST API is the right surface: **`POST /api/v1/dags/{dag_id}/dagRuns`** with an empty JSON body triggers a manual run; **basic auth** works because the harness sets `REDACTED,session` (the TF comment says it's kept exactly for DataQ's poller). The admin user clears the required role (POST dagRuns needs ≥User).
- One documented gotcha worth the check: **a run created on a paused DAG never executes** — it just queues. So the script PATCHes `is_paused: false` (with `update_mask`) before triggering, idempotently.
- Run status polls `GET /api/v1/dags/{id}/dagRuns/{run_id}` until it leaves `queued`/`running`.

**Script updates** (`harness_window.sh`, syntax-checked):
- New **`--dags` flag** on `run`/`window`: unpauses + manually triggers the three cron DAGs sequentially (`flow_a_snowflake_load` → `flow_a_uc_reference` → `flow_b_medallion`, mirroring their cron ordering) and reports each terminal state. Credentials come from the harness Terraform outputs (`airflow_admin_user`/`_password` — verified they resolve locally, no Key Vault dependency).
- `flow_a_payments_event` is deliberately **not** force-triggered: it's `schedule=None` and expects an Event-Grid `conf['blob_url']` payload — it fires naturally when the mockdata jobs land files while the harness is awake. And `flow_a_snowflake_load` will start failing once Snowflake lapses (#588), same caveat as `--adf`.
- Flags now combine: `harness_window.sh window --adf --dags` is the full one-shot.

Also, closing the loop on your previous question with test evidence: a suspended job **refuses** manual starts (verified — the API errors with "Job is suspended"), so I fixed `run` to resume → start → re-suspend, and proved the sequence live: execution `mockdata-feedback-d7wvjl5` ran to **Succeeded** and the job is back to suspended (with a 409-retry added, since suspend races the start's provisioning). The only path not yet exercised end-to-end is the DAG trigger itself, since that requires waking Airflow (~CAD 0.70/hour) — it'll get its first real validation on your next test window or the W3 final E2E.

all the harness jobs are stopped, right?
then lets test out the script now.

Yes — verified stopped earlier (`status` showed all four apps `Stopped`, both ADF triggers `Stopped`, jobs suspended). Let's run the full cycle: `window --adf --dags` — that exercises every path (wake → mockdata manual runs → DAG triggers → ADF pipelines → sleep), and doubles as a Flow A/B/C live check while Snowflake may still be alive. Expect 30–60 minutes end-to-end, ~CAD 1 of burn. Launching in the background.

content.text:
The full window test **passed end-to-end, 11.5 minutes, exit 0** — every leg of the script is now verified on real infra:

| Phase | Result |
|---|---|
| Wake (redis → Airflow `/health` → workers → trigger → ADF triggers) | up in 2.7 min |
| 5 mockdata jobs (manual executions) | all `Succeeded`, re-suspended after |
| 3 cron DAGs via REST (`snowflake_load`, `uc_reference`, `flow_b_medallion`) | all `success` |
| 2 ADF pipelines (`pl_flow_a_customers`, `pl_flow_a_orders`) | both `Succeeded` |
| Sleep (triggers → jobs → apps, reverse order) | everything back to `Stopped`, each verified |

Cost of the test: ~11 minutes awake ≈ **CAD 0.15**. Burn is back to the ~CAD 4.5/day app-only baseline.

Two bonuses from the run: Snowflake is confirmed **still alive** — that was an unplanned final green run of Flows A and B before the lapse (the W1 items #194/#195/#587 still deserve their day-1 slot while this holds). And DataQ's 10-minute poller will have ingested these ADF/Airflow runs, so the Results → pipeline-runs tab should show fresh entries from 19:42–19:50 UTC if you want an eyeball check in the app.

The script is fully production-ready for your ad-hoc windows: `harness_window.sh window --adf --dags` for everything, or `start` / `run` / `stop` piecemeal. The wake runbook comment on [#590](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/590) already points to it.

content.text:
`low effort → 1 diff pass → no verify → ≤4 findings`

## Turn 1 — read

One tool call: read the unified diff (`git diff @{upstream}...HEAD; git diff HEAD`
to cover both committed and uncommitted changes, or `git diff main...HEAD` /
the target passed as an argument). Skip test/fixture
hunks (`test/`, `spec/`, `__tests__/`, `*_test.*`, `*.test.*`,
`fixtures/`, `testdata/`) — test-file changes are not reviewed at this level.
No subagents, no full-file reads.

## Turn 2 — findings

Flag runtime-correctness bugs visible from the hunk alone: inverted/wrong
condition, off-by-one, null/undefined deref where adjacent lines show the value
can be absent, removed guard, falsy-zero check, missing `await`,
wrong-variable copy-paste, error swallowed in a catch that should propagate.
Also flag — still from the hunk alone — new code that duplicates an existing
helper visible in the diff context, and dead code the diff leaves behind.

Do **not** flag style, naming, perf, missing tests, or anything outside the
hunk.

Output at most **4 findings**, most-severe first, one line each:
`path/to/file.ext:123 — what's wrong and the concrete failure`. If nothing
qualifies, output exactly `(none)`.

#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail
OWNER=TheurgicDuke771
REDACTED
REDACTED
REDACTED
TODO_OPT=f75ad846
# W4=0f8ce3e2 W5=37c1a30f W6=12dd9999
declare -a ITEMS=(
  "0f8ce3e2 520"
  "37c1a30f 424" "37c1a30f 349"
  "12dd9999 278" "12dd9999 322" "12dd9999 571" "12dd9999 541" "12dd9999 306"
)
for entry in "${ITEMS[@]}"; do
  opt=${entry% *}; num=${entry#* }
  item_id=$(gh project item-add 1 --owner "$OWNER" \
    --url "https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/$num" --format json -q '.id')
  gh project item-edit --id "$item_id" --project-id "$PROJECT_ID" \
    --field-id "$WEEK_FIELD" --single-select-option-id "$opt" >/dev/null
  gh project item-edit --id "$item_id" --project-id "$PROJECT_ID" \
    --field-id "$STATUS_FIELD" --single-select-option-id "$TODO_OPT" >/dev/null
  echo "#$num wired"
done

content.text:
`low effort → 1 diff pass → no verify → ≤4 findings`

## Turn 1 — read

One tool call: read the unified diff (`git diff @{upstream}...HEAD; git diff HEAD`
to cover both committed and uncommitted changes, or `git diff main...HEAD` /
the target passed as an argument). Skip test/fixture
hunks (`test/`, `spec/`, `__tests__/`, `*_test.*`, `*.test.*`,
`fixtures/`, `testdata/`) — test-file changes are not reviewed at this level.
No subagents, no full-file reads.

## Turn 2 — findings

Flag runtime-correctness bugs visible from the hunk alone: inverted/wrong
condition, off-by-one, null/undefined deref where adjacent lines show the value
can be absent, removed guard, falsy-zero check, missing `await`,
wrong-variable copy-paste, error swallowed in a catch that should propagate.
Also flag — still from the hunk alone — new code that duplicates an existing
helper visible in the diff context, and dead code the diff leaves behind.

Do **not** flag style, naming, perf, missing tests, or anything outside the
hunk.

Output at most **4 findings**, most-severe first, one line each:
`path/to/file.ext:123 — what's wrong and the concrete failure`. If nothing
qualifies, output exactly `(none)`.

input.command: git diff main...HEAD; git diff HEAD

content.text:
CI is running on PR [#600](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/600) — I'll merge when green. Status of the remap so far, all live on GitHub:

- **Milestone renamed** to `v1.1 Backlog` (description updated to explain the flow: issues graduate from it to a `v1.1 Week N` milestone when scheduled).
- **8 week-fit issues moved and fully wired** (milestone + project `v1.1 week` field + epic #597 checklists): #520 → W4 (flat-file freshness/volume, same engine code as the schema_drift work) · #424, #349 → W5 (results-surface week) · #278, #322, #571, #541, #306 → W6 (test-hardening + small-bug batches). **31 issues stay backlogged** — compliance, alerting nits, the frontend-refactor batch, and the big next-cycle features, per the roadmap themes.
- **Planning correction found en route:** #492 (ADF webhook delivery) had been scheduled into W3, but it actually **closed 2026-07-02** — the metric-alert delivery path shipped and was verified live during the W7 smoke. Re-homed to its Week-7 milestone; W3 is now 3 rows.
- **Final week loads:** W1 6 · W2 5 · W3 3 · W4 5 · W5 5 · W6 12 — 66 open issues total (34 scheduled + epic + 31 backlog), verified against GitHub.
- **Docs PR #600** updates progress.md plus every live reference to the old milestone name — including the `gh-issue-from-finding` skill, which files issues by milestone *title* and would have silently broken on the rename. The frozen `progress-v1.md` keeps the historical name. Review caught two count inconsistencies (W4 subtotal, project-wiring count), fixed.
Can't we map these 31 backlogs also?, lets try to group them first

Good instinct — let me group the 31 first, because they cluster cleanly, and a few genuinely can't be done this cycle. Here's the grouping with my proposed disposition:

**Mappable into weeks (21):**

| Group | Issues | Proposed week + why |
|---|---|---|
| **A. Alerting hardening batch** (Theme 5) | #386, #387, #388, #389 (four P3 nits, one batch PR) + #416 (alert enrichment) | **W2** — #389's channel-neutral rename is literally a vendor-neutrality item (W2's theme), and all five touch the same routing/dedup/suppression code |
| **A′. MCP authz** | #488 (workspace-admin visibility in MCP tools + schedules) | **W2** — rides the PATs (W1) + #584 MCP work |
| **B. Frontend refactor/cleanup batch** (Theme 10) | #197, #199, #204, #229, #236, #237, #326 (extract-helper refactors + dead-UI fixes) | **W3** — the lean ops week (teardown is mostly waiting on Azure); doing these *before* W5 means the new trend/results UI reuses the helpers instead of duplicating more |
| **C. Profiler & check-model** | #476 (profiler casing/CSV-delimiter), #124 (DQ-dimension classification) | **W4** — profiler/flat-file engine and the check editor are both open that week for schema_drift |
| **D. Results & connections UX** | #345 (PDF export), #283 (version restore/revert), #351 (Test Connection button) | **W5** — the results-surface week |
| **E. Progress/guards/design** | #318 (per-check incremental progress — becomes natural once #595's partitioned execution exists), #457 (index-predicate drift guard), #310 (history/audit ADR — overlaps the #596 G-d design doc) | **W6** |

**Genuinely next-cycle (10) — I'd keep these on `v1.1 Backlog` as named v1.2 seeds:**

- **F. Compliance cluster** #431–#435: a coherent v1.2 opening theme (pairs with the G-d incident design + Theme 3). Two are also *blocked now*: #433's warehouse-tag source (Snowflake) dies this week, and #435 asserts encryption on IaC we're destroying in W3.
- **G. Week-sized features**: #529 (MCP expansion — needs its own week), #286 (Iceberg), #244 (suite-on-suite triggering), #466 (browsing — the ADLS half dies with Azure), #505 (AWS/GCP IaC — no target subscription exists).

Resulting loads if applied: W1 6 · W2 11 · W3 10 · W4 7 · W5 8 · W6 15 — heavy but mostly small items (v1 averaged ~24/week at your pace); W6 is the one I'd watch.

          "Redaction parity with the API verified"],

          "Edit-permission-gated; action available in the version-history drawer; tests"],
    351: ["Test Connection works on the New/Edit page BEFORE save (draft-connection test endpoint)",
          "Draft secrets are not persisted by the test call; clear success/failure surface; tests"],
    # ---------------- W6 ----------------
    595: ["Sampling strategies (head-N / random) + per-partition/file batching for the flat-file runner; UC pushes monitors + SQL-able subsets down before any collect",
          "Size probe + configurable hard cap → run ends `error` with a clear message instead of worker OOM",
          "Sampled-ness recorded on the result and visible in the UI (a pass on a sample says so)",
          "Volume test vs the #587 Snowflake baseline documented"],
    327: ["Profiler top-values collected in batched queries (O(1)–O(2) per table, not per column)",
          "Before/after timing recorded; behavior unchanged (tests)"],
    323: ["Retention sweep uses an index + batched deletes/updates; runtime on a large seeded `results` table measured",
          "No lock-contention regression (documented check)"],
    596: ["Design doc merged: incident objects (lifecycle, dedup vs alerts), asset-entity anchoring (OpenLineage naming), lineage pull-first slice, ownership routing, RCA-card-as-payload",

    563: ["Every spike survivor triaged: killing test added OR explicit wontfix rationale on the issue",

          "Green across ≥20 consecutive runs (CI or local loop) — evidence linked"],

    571: ["Pre-dispatch-failure runs show a truthful `checks_total` (real total or explicit —/null semantics), consistent with the progress endpoint",

    # ---------------- W7 stretch ----------------

          "Workspace-admin can query the audit trail; retention policy documented",

          "User-data inventory documented; erasure exercised on a demo user in a test env"],
    433: ["Column classification consumed from warehouse tags (UC tags now; Snowflake documented-blocked post-lapse)",
          "Redaction reads tags over name heuristics; source-precedence rules tested"],
    434: ["Deployment region/residency asserted in config and surfaced; external-transfer vectors (LLM seam) documented + gated"],
    435: ["Encryption-at-rest asserted in whatever IaC exists at build time; CMK option designed/documented (build blocked while no cloud target)"],

    286: ["Spike: Iceberg v2/v3 read path decided (format next to csv/parquet vs engine-level) and recorded",
          "A check runs green against an Iceberg table in the chosen shape"],
    244: ["`trigger_bindings` supports suite-on-suite (run suite B when suite A completes successfully)",
          "Cycle/self-trigger protection enforced + tested; UI can author the binding"],
    466: ["S3 container/prefix browser + UC catalog→schema→table picker in the connection/suite-target UX (ADLS documented-blocked post-Azure)",
          "Listing is authz-safe and paginated; explicit-target entry still works"],
    505: ["Terraform for one non-Azure cloud (AWS or GCP) deploys the full stack behind the ADR-0010/0028 seams",
          "Deployment guide section added (blocked until a target subscription exists — record the blocker if still true at pickup)"],
}

def run(args, inp=None):
    return subprocess.run(args, capture_output=True, text=True, input=inp)

changed, skipped, failed = [], [], []
for num, crits in AC.items():
    r = run(["gh", "api", f"repos/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/{num}"])
    if r.returncode != 0:
        failed.append((num, r.stderr[:100])); continue
    body = json.loads(r.stdout).get("body") or ""
    if "acceptance criteria" in body.lower():
        skipped.append(num); continue
    section = "\n\n## Acceptance criteria\n\n" + "\n".join(f"- [ ] {c}" for c in crits) + \
              "\n\n_(AC added at v1.1 planning, 2026-07-04.)_"
    payload = json.dumps({"body": body + section})
    r2 = run(["gh", "api", "-X", "PATCH", f"repos/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/{num}", "--input", "-"], inp=payload)
    (changed if r2.returncode == 0 else failed).append(num if r2.returncode == 0 else (num, r2.stderr[:100]))

print(f"AC added: {len(changed)} -> {changed}")
print(f"skipped (already had AC): {skipped}")
print(f"failed: {failed}")

input.command:
python3 - <<'EOF'
import json, subprocess
AC = {
    318: ["Run progress reports per-check/per-partition increments instead of binary 0%→100% (natural once #595's partitioned execution lands — ride that work)",
          "Live-progress drawer reflects the increments; progress endpoint contract unchanged or versioned",
          "Tests cover multi-partition progress sequencing"],
    457: ["A guard (test or CHECK) fails loudly if a 3rd OrchestrationProvider is added without updating the partial-index/predicate set",
          "Documented in the orchestration-provider extension notes"],
}
for num, crits in AC.items():
    r = subprocess.run(["gh","api",f"repos/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/{num}"],capture_output=True,text=True)
    body = json.loads(r.stdout).get("body") or ""
    if "acceptance criteria" in body.lower():
        print(f"#{num}: already present"); continue
    section = "\n\n## Acceptance criteria\n\n" + "\n".join(f"- [ ] {c}" for c in crits) + "\n\n_(AC added at v1.1 planning, 2026-07-04.)_"
    payload = json.dumps({"body": body + section})
    r2 = subprocess.run(["gh","api","-X","PATCH",f"repos/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/{num}","--input","-"],capture_output=True,text=True,input=payload)
    print(f"#{num}: {'AC added' if r2.returncode==0 else 'FAILED'}")
EOF
